nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef flats and lagoons; found attached to rocks and rubble in areas with moderate light and low to moderate water movement

decorPreferences: rocky substrate or rubble for attachment
lower rockwork or sandy bottom placement
spacing from neighboring corals to prevent chemical aggression
keep separate from aggressive LPS or SPS corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis. Supplemental feeding 1–2 times weekly with meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, or zooplankton is generally recommended to support growth and reproduction. Foods should be small enough to be captured by the oral disc (roughly half a mysis shrimp in size). Amino acid supplementation has been reported to be beneficial.

avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals without spacing (sweeper tentacle contact)
fast-growing encrusting corals that may overgrow

summary: Ricordea florida is a Caribbean mushroom corallimorph prized for its bumpy, jewel-toned disc polyps. Generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly corals; tolerates low-moderate lighting and low flow.
careNotes:
This entry covers Ricordea florida, the Caribbean species. The Indo-Pacific sibling species R. yuma is catalogued separately as sw-coral-092 (yuma-mushroom-coral); R. yuma is generally considered more sensitive to water quality and is better suited for intermediate keepers.

Ricordea florida is distinguished from other mushroom corals (Discosoma/Actinodiscus, Rhodactis) by its distinctive short, club-shaped or berry-like tentacles covering the entire oral disc, giving it a bumpy, jeweled appearance. It is the only genus in the monotypic family Ricordeidae, order Corallimorpharia.

Lighting: Generally thrives in 50–150 PAR. Moderate lighting tends to bring out the best coloration; metal halide or very intense reef lighting at close range can cause bleaching. New specimens should be acclimated gradually to avoid light shock.

Flow: Low to moderate flow is generally advised. High or direct flow tends to cause the polyp to retract or detach. Gentle, indirect circulation is preferred.

Placement: Lower rockwork or sandy substrate is typically recommended. Because Ricordea are mobile corallimorphs capable of slow movement, they should be placed with adequate spacing from other corals to prevent chemical allelopathy and physical contact. Running activated carbon is generally recommended in tanks with high mushroom populations.

Aggression: Semi-aggressive; tends to use sweeper-tentacle-like contact and chemical allelopathy to compete for space. Adequate spacing (several centimeters) from neighboring corals is important.

Water quality: Less demanding than stony corals regarding calcium and alkalinity precision, but Ricordea florida have been reported to react more negatively to sudden parameter swings than to a stable system that is slightly outside textbook ranges. Rapid salinity changes, alkalinity swings, or repeated lighting changes can cause shrinking, fading, or detachment.

Propagation: Reproduces naturally via longitudinal fission (the polyp develops two mouths and splits), pedal laceration, or can be manually fragged. Reproduction tends to be slow to moderate in the home aquarium.

Trade notes: Sold in many color forms including green, orange, purple, rainbow, and multicolor morphs. All are the same species with the same care requirements. High-color morphs tend to command premium prices in the reef hobby.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity occurs primarily via longitudinal fission (the polyp splits in two), pedal laceration (small clones detach from the base), or manual fragging. Natural splitting is slow; fragging by cutting is practiced by advanced hobbyists. Sexual reproduction is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Green Ricordea Mushroom Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for care level, temperament, size range, temperature, pH, salinity, kH, flow, placement, and diet overview.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Ricordea Mushroom Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked PAR range (75–150), calcium (400–450 ppm), care level (beginner), and temperament. Temperature range given as 77–84°F — broader than other sources; conservative lower bound of 22°C retained.

additional.name: Build Your Aquarium – Ricordea Coral Care Sheet
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F = 22–26°C), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (8.0–8.24), kH (9–12), calcium (350–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1350 ppm). Native range confirmed as Indo-Pacific and Caribbean (R. florida = Caribbean).

additional.name: AquariumBreeder – How to Care for Ricordea Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.021–1.025), kH (8–12), adult size (2–5 inches / 5–13 cm), min tank 10 gal (~40 L), feeding 1–2× weekly. R. florida native range confirmed as Atlantic/Caribbean.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Ricordea Mushroom Coral Care
additional.notes: PAR calibration: medium light defined as 50–150 PAR; confirms R. florida as hardier beginner species vs. R. yuma. Feeding: recommends meaty foods ~half-mysis size.
